video_title: The New Stablecoin Race, Hyperliquid’s Growth & Solana’s Revival
gist: The Blockworks Research team reads Strategy's new digital-credit framework as a possible high-timeframe bottom signal while warning forced BTC selling remains the tail risk; dissects a new TradFi-heavy consortium stablecoin that shares treasury yield with protocols; and argues tokenized equities plus Hyperliquid priority fees are structurally more durable revenue than the fading meme-coin trade.

themes.theme: Strategy's digital credit framework as bottom signal

insights.claim: Strategy announced a digital credit capital framework with five levers: holding at least one year of dividend coverage in cash, raising the STRC (stretch) dividend to 12% from 11.5%, $1B in authorized preferred repurchases, up to $1B in common stock buybacks, and a 'Bitcoin monetization program' (i.e. selling BTC) to fund those obligations.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete new balance-sheet defense mechanics to model for MSTR common and its preferred stack; watch whether buybacks and BTC sales actually hit the tape.
insights.quote: a Bitcoin monetization program which really was just a nice way of saying uh we we're going to sell Bitcoin to cover these other other four obligations here

insights.claim: STRC (stretch) trading down to a 70 handle is effectively credit spreads blowing out, and just as in equity markets that tends to coincide with high-timeframe lows, so its rally back is a constructive spread-tightening signal for Strategy's instruments.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Use STRC price / implied credit spread as a real-time sentiment gauge for the whole MSTR complex and a potential bottoming tell.
insights.quote: when stretch trades down um to the the 70s handle, that's basically credit spreads blowing out

insights.claim: The market may be over-optimistically pricing in that Strategy will never have to sell BTC now that Saylor has signaled willingness to defend the price, but if he actually has to start selling Bitcoin in size things can get really ugly.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Forced BTC liquidation remains the underpriced tail risk for both MSTR and spot Bitcoin; the guidance defers rather than removes it.
insights.quote: if he needs to start selling BTC in size like things can get really ugly

insights.claim: The Strategy framework is only a band-aid: if Bitcoin trades sideways for 6-12 months, Strategy will likely have to tap the BTC market again for another ~$1B to fund the stretch dividend, whereas a rising BTC lets them hit the ATM instead and leave the Bitcoin untouched.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: The BTC-sale overhang is path-dependent on Bitcoin's price; a flat tape re-arms the same balance-sheet fear that just cleared.
insights.quote: band-aid fix for now, but all the problems that came up over the past month could easily come up again if if Bitcoin continues to draw down

insights.claim: Strategy functions like the de facto central bank or bond issuer for Bitcoin, so its forward guidance on defending preferreds shapes credit conditions across the BTC-treasury-company complex.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Treat Strategy's capital-policy signals like macro/rate guidance for the Bitcoin-proxy-equity sector.

themes.theme: TradFi consortium stablecoin sharing treasury yield

insights.claim: A new consortium stablecoin (Open Standard, tied to Tempo) launched with roughly 140 partners including Visa, Stripe, MasterCard, Amex, BlackRock, BNY, Standard Chartered, Coinbase, Solana and Base.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: A payments-incumbent-backed stablecoin is a competitive threat to USDC/USDT distribution; track which merchants and rails adopt it.

insights.claim: The consortium stablecoin's differentiator is that it automatically shares all treasury yield with the protocols that use it, removing the opportunity cost protocols bear holding USDC and extending the trend from the Circle-Coinbase 50/50 split to Hyperliquid capturing 90% of its USDC yield.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Yield-sharing is becoming table stakes; issuers that don't rebate treasury yield to integrators risk losing protocol distribution.
insights.quote: the writing was already on the wall that these protocols and users are going to want to internalize the treasury yield more and more

insights.claim: Because current legislation blocks paying stablecoin yield directly to users, protocols with treasury-yield revenue-share agreements can route that yield to users as 'rewards,' effectively giving holders a portion of treasury yield back despite the ban.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: A regulatory-arbitrage path to yield-bearing dollars that could accelerate stablecoin adoption; watch for rewards-wrapped yield products.

insights.claim: The consortium's ~140 launch names already represent over 99% of global card networks, 70%+ of payment processors, 60%+ of merchant checkout software, 70%+ of enterprise payment infrastructure and 80%+ of crypto payment infrastructure, making this issuer more likely to crack payments than the single-issuer entrants of the past year.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Pre-existing incumbent distribution is the moat; this consortium starts with the merchant and processor footprint most new stablecoins lack.
insights.quote: these names account for over 99% of global card networks, 70% plus of payment processors, 60% plus of merchant checkout software, 70% plus of enterprise payment infrastructure, and 80% plus of crypto payment infrastructure

insights.claim: The consortium mirrors USDG / the Global Dollar Consortium but with an inverted partner set — TradFi payment processors here versus crypto/DeFi-native names (Aave, Robinhood, OKX) in USDG — so each may dominate its own vertical rather than the whole market.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: Expect a fragmented stablecoin market segmented by distribution niche, not a single winner.

insights.claim: With ~140 launch partners the consortium's governance could start to resemble a DAO, and too many members may struggle to agree, creating coordination risk over the next year of development.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Coordination/governance friction is the main bear case on the consortium model despite its distribution advantage.

insights.claim: Robinhood launched Robinhood Chain mainnet with tokenized stock tokens, expanded perpetual futures, and an earn product powered by Morpho offering an estimated 7% APY on USDG.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Robinhood is verticalizing crypto rails; the Morpho-powered earn could pull material stablecoin deposits into Morpho.

insights.claim: Robinhood Chain's tokenized equities and perps are EU/rest-of-world only and won't be material to Hood revenue near-term since ~98% of its customer base is US-based; the real upside case is US regulation easing, which could let Robinhood deploy these products to its ~28 million US customers in roughly a one-week rollout for a material revenue boost.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: The Hood bull case is a regulatory option — pre-built products waiting on a US switch-flip; size the position to that catalyst.
insights.quote: all these products that they're building kind of rest of world, they could enable for their entire US like 28 million something customer base in, you know, one click or what you know, a one week deployment type of outcome

insights.claim: Coinbase has not been a great operator or executor and it shows in the stock and business, while Robinhood has executed better on comparable Morpho-integrated earn/borrow product rollouts despite arriving later, making Hood the preferred name.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: Relative-value long Hood / skeptical Coinbase execution thesis for anyone comparing the two crypto-fintech equities.
insights.quote: that they've just not been great operators and executors and I think I don't know that reflects in the stock price

themes.theme: Lighter vs Hyperliquid for Robinhood perps

insights.claim: Robinhood chose Lighter over Hyperliquid as its perps partner because Lighter agreed to spin up a dedicated instance on Robinhood Chain so execution and settlement value stays with Robinhood, whereas neutral Hyperliquid would only offer builder codes and won't build a special instance.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid's neutrality costs it walled-garden integrations; value-capture-hungry platforms will route to more accommodating venues like Lighter.
insights.quote: hyperliquid is a more neutral platform. They're not going to bend over backwards and create a new instance for you

insights.claim: Lighter is giving Robinhood 11 million tokens as integration incentives and Robinhood's Vlad Tenev is personally an investor in Lighter, both reinforcing the partnership choice at a cost to LIT holders.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: The 11M-token incentive is a real dilution cost for LIT holders; weigh go-to-market volume gains against token emissions.
insights.quote: the fact that Lighter's giving them 11 million tokens as incentives definitely doesn't hurt

insights.claim: Robinhood could eventually pull perps fully in-house the way it did with prediction markets after starting with Kalshi, which would cap Lighter's long-term upside even though the integration is more material for Lighter near-term.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: Lighter's Robinhood flow may be a temporary boost; model the risk of Robinhood internalizing the order book later.

insights.claim: Priority fees are the internalization of on-chain arbitrage/liquidation opportunities — because an L1 controls sequencing it can force competing searcher bots to pay a share of expected return for favorable placement — and on both Solana and ETH roughly 80% of REV comes from priority fees rather than base fees.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Priority-fee capture, not base fees, is where L1 revenue concentrates; Hyperliquid adopting it opens its most lucrative monetization layer.
insights.quote: priority fees are basically the internalization of blockchain opportunities that searchers, traders need to pay to get access to the opportunities

insights.claim: Hyperliquid processes passive liquidity orders (adds/cancels) first each block so quotes can refresh, then lets aggressive takers attach a priority fee of 1-8 basis points of executed volume, with each basis point buying about a 45-millisecond time improvement — internalizing latency wars that leak externally in TradFi without letting flow pick off stale market-maker liquidity.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: A neutral fee design that monetizes latency without harming market makers — a template other order-book chains may copy.
insights.quote: allow traders to attach a priority fee, ranging from 1 to 8 basis points of executed volume, and each basis points buys the trader around 45 millisecond improvement in their time

insights.claim: Hyperliquid priority fees are early — only ~244 wallets have ever paid, they are about 6% of base fees over the trailing 7 days and roughly $100k/day — but top traders in top markets are already handing back about 30% of their edge to the platform.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Concrete baseline metrics to track priority-fee ramp as a new HYPE revenue line.
insights.quote: It was around 30% of their edge was going back into the platform because of the priority fees that they paid

insights.claim: Hyperliquid priority fees could grow roughly 10x from current levels but will not exceed base fees, unlike on general-purpose L1s.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Sizes the upside of the new fee line for HYPE holders — meaningful but capped below base-fee revenue.
insights.quote: I definitely think it's still very early days and we can expect it to grow like 10x from here

insights.claim: Priority fees are highest in HIP-3 markets like SpaceX where multiple market makers compete for the same less-efficient edge — there priority fees exceed the base fees Hyperliquid earns — while hyper-efficient markets like BTC generate only ~$14k because the edge and competition are too small, offering a monetization path for HIP-3's 50/50 deploy-fee-share model.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Long-tail / less-liquid HIP-3 equity markets are where priority-fee revenue accrues; watch HIP-3 deployment as the growth driver.
insights.quote: in like HIP-3 markets like SpaceX and other equities, the priority fees are actually more than the base fees that Hyperliquid earns

insights.claim: Unlike L1s where priority fees go to validators, Hyperliquid's priority fees accrue to the protocol itself and are paid in HYPE and burned, similar to its other fee sinks.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Priority-fee growth is directly deflationary for HYPE via burn, strengthening the token's value accrual.
insights.quote: it's just going to be paid in hype and burn similar to the system fund and other fee sinks in Hyperliquid

themes.theme: Solana trenches — tokenized equities displacing memes

insights.claim: Tokenized equities flipped meme coins in Solana volume about 1-2 weeks ago, and even off a base of only ~4 tradable stocks (via Backpack and xStocks) plus names like SpaceX and AI-infrastructure plays, the activity is proving sustainable week to week.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Tokenized equity flow on Solana is a new, less sentiment-dependent volume source; track it as a structural DEX revenue driver.

insights.claim: Tokenized assets on Solana will likely reach 10-20x the volume of meme coins within a year because they are far more sustainable activity, while memes stay volatile and sentiment-dependent.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: A concrete, falsifiable call for reallocating Solana on-chain volume exposure toward tokenized equities over memes.
insights.quote: in a year's time, I reckon tokenized assets are going to be 10 20 x the volume of memes on Solana

insights.claim: No one actually wants to trade meme coins — traders were pushed into them only after project tokens became efficient value-extraction vehicles for VCs and market makers, and memes then suffered the same fate as insiders learned to multi-wallet and dump (e.g. Trump coin extracting ~$1B), so leveraged longs on instruments that are going up are more attractive.
insights.speaker: Shonda (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Structural bear case on meme-coin volume as a durable revenue stream; edge has migrated to leverage on trending majors and equities.
insights.quote: my controversial opinion is that no one wants to trade memes. And we were all basically forced into it

insights.claim: Meme trading died because once the profit opportunity became obvious, insiders shifted from naive single-wallet spot buying to multi-walleting and bundling supply across ~100 wallets (via apps like Axiom) to dump on traders, and that pure state cannot be recovered.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: Explains the structural decay in meme volume/revenue; the trade doesn't reset to its early high-multiple regime.
insights.quote: the applications that target like multi-walleting and like bundling up of supply across 100 wallets and then dumping the supply like kind of unknowingly on on people

insights.claim: Speculative retail manias just change form each cycle — NFTs, then meme coins, then AI-agent tokens (essentially memes with an AI narrative) — and a full meme season will likely recur 5-10 years out as the market forgets its hard lessons.
insights.speaker: Danny (co-host)
insights.so_what: Treat speculative categories as rolling bubbles; position for the next wrapper rather than expecting memes specifically to return soon.
insights.quote: there's always a bull bull market somewhere

insights.claim: The durable edge in trading is table selection — avoid competing against billion-dollar HFT desks in efficient markets like BTC — which is why tokenized equities on Solana and equity perpetual futures on Hyperliquid/Lighter offer better risk-adjusted returns than options that bleed beta and decay.
insights.speaker: Luke (host)
insights.so_what: Preference for cash-flow-backed tokenized equities and leveraged equity perps over options and memes as the higher-EV structural trade.
insights.quote: you don't want to step into the ring and box with Mike Tyson, right?

insights.claim: Ansem's meme token running to nearly $200M market cap is a read-through indicator of euphoric market sentiment, echoing how prior tokens like Dogwifhat rallied at market tops.
insights.speaker: Sam (guest, Blockworks Research)
insights.so_what: Use marquee meme-coin runs as a contrarian sentiment/froth gauge for broader market timing.
